2.3.5.DSDT.3.standardStatement: Demonstrate effective refusal skills of alcohol, e-cigarettes, vaping products, cannabis products, opioids, and other substances that can negatively impact health.
2.3.5.DSDT.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Recall three ways to say no when offered a substance like alcohol or a vaping product.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes three refusal phrases on an index card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name three ways to refuse when someone offers you something that could hurt your health.' Each student writes three short refusal phrases on an index card. Cards go on a class chart sorted into spoken refusals, body-language refusals, and leaving the situation.

understand-1.objective: Describe what makes a refusal clear and firm rather than weak.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student marks four sample refusals on a handout as firm or weak and writes why.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight written refusal lines, four firm and four weak. Students label each firm or weak and add one word explaining the call. Whole-class review names the features of a firm refusal: a clear no, eye contact, and an exit.

understand-2.objective: Explain why naming a reason makes a refusal stronger.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a refusal that pairs a no with one honest reason.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student picks a substance from the standard and writes one refusal that joins a clear no with a real reason, such as 'No, I have a game tomorrow.' Pairs trade cards and check that each refusal has both parts. Two volunteers read theirs aloud.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the steps of a refusal in the student's own words.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student lists the refusal steps in order on a strip of paper.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a four-step refusal model: say no, give a reason, suggest something else, and leave if needed. Students restate the four steps in their own words on a sentence strip without copying the poster. Strips post on the wall as a class reference.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the same refusal changes depending on who is offering.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes how one refusal would shift for a friend versus a stranger.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one offer scenario. Each student writes two versions of a refusal, one for a close friend and one for an older student they barely know, and notes what changed and why. Small groups compare and pick the clearest pair to share.

apply-1.objective: Use a refusal phrase in a quick scripted exchange.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student delivers one scripted refusal to a partner who reads the offer line.
apply-1.sampleTask: Partners get a two-line script: one reads the offer, the other reads a refusal from the class chart. They run it twice and switch roles. Guide circulates and taps pairs to perform their line for the class.

apply-2.objective: Carry out a refusal in an unscripted role-play of a common offer.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student improvises a refusal in a 30-second role-play without a script.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide draws a scenario card describing a realistic offer at a park or a friend's house. One partner makes the offer; the other refuses using a clear no, a reason, and an exit. Pairs run two rounds, then write one sentence on what worked.

apply-3.objective: Apply refusal steps to a multi-pressure scenario where the offer repeats.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student holds a refusal through three repeated offers in a role-play.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a role-play where one student keeps re-offering after each no. The refuser must restate the no, hold steady, and leave by the third try. Partners run it, then list two pressure tactics they noticed and how the refusal handled each.

apply-4.objective: Use refusal skills in a scenario set in an unfamiliar place with adult bystanders present.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names which bystander adult they would involve during the role-play.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide presents a scenario at a community event where adults are nearby. Students role-play refusing and then identify and approach a 'trusted adult' actor for help. Each writes who they chose and why that adult was a good choice.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the pressure tactic used in a written offer.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student labels the tactic in three short offer scripts.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out three offer scripts. Students label each with the tactic in play, such as flattery, dare, or 'everyone does it.' Pairs check matches against a tactics word bank, then add one tactic the bank is missing.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two refusals to determine which holds up better under pressure.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger refusal and cites two reasons from the text.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives two written refusals to the same repeated offer. Students decide which holds up better and underline two lines that support the choice. Small groups debate, then post their pick with evidence on a class T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break a refusal exchange into the moves each speaker makes.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps an exchange into offer moves and refusal moves on a flowchart.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a six-line exchange and map it on a flowchart, labeling each line as a pressure move or a refusal move. They mark the turn where the refuser took control. Pairs compare flowcharts and resolve any differences.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how setting and audience change which refusal move works.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student matches three settings to the refusal move that fits each best.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Guide gives three settings: a bus, a party, and a sports field. Students decide which refusal move fits each setting best and write why the setting drove the choice. Groups build a chart linking setting features to refusal moves.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's role-play refusal against a clear-no, reason, and exit rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a recorded or live refusal on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students watch a partner's refusal role-play and score it on a three-row rubric: clear no, honest reason, and exit. They write one specific line of feedback for the strongest row and one for the weakest. Partners swap scores and discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend a recommendation for which refusal strategy a younger student should learn first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one strategy and backs it with two reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide poses the question: which refusal move should a third grader learn first? Each student writes a short claim naming one move and gives two reasons tied to how third graders act. Groups rank their claims and present the top pick to the class.

create-1.objective: Generate a list of fresh refusal lines for a class reference poster.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student contributes two original refusal lines to the class poster.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student writes two refusal lines that are not already on the class chart and adds them to a shared poster. The class reads through and circles the five clearest. The poster stays up as a reference for later role-plays.

create-2.objective: Design a short comic strip that shows a character refusing under pressure.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a three-panel comic with a refusal in the final panel.
create-2.sampleTask: Students draw a three-panel comic: an offer, a pressure move, and a clear refusal with an exit. Speech bubbles must show the no and a reason. Comics post on the wall and a gallery walk picks ones that show the steps clearly.

create-3.objective: Build a role-play script that walks through a repeated-offer refusal.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a written script with at least two re-offers and a held refusal.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs write an original script where a character refuses, faces two re-offers, and exits firmly. The script must label each pressure tactic in the margin. Pairs perform their scripts and the class names the tactics they spotted.

create-4.objective: Produce a refusal-skills mini-guide for fifth graders that combines steps, tactics, and examples.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a one-page guide with steps, two tactics, and a worked example.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a one-page mini-guide for new fifth graders covering the refusal steps, two pressure tactics to watch for, and one worked example exchange. They draft, peer-review against a checklist, and revise. Finished guides go in a class binder for younger classes.

2.3.5.DSDT.4.standardStatement: Demonstrate strategies for seeking assistance for someone impacted by an individual who is struggling with the use/abuse of alcohol or drugs.
2.3.5.DSDT.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name three trusted adults a student could tell when a family member's drug or alcohol use is hurting someone.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student lists three named or role-based adults on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Who are three adults you could go to for help with a hard family problem?' Students write three on a card, using roles like school counselor, nurse, or coach if they prefer not to name people. Cards stay private; a class tally counts how many list a school adult.

understand-1.objective: Describe what a school counselor or nurse does to help a student with a hard home situation.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student matches three school helpers to what each one can do.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a card set listing school helpers and a separate set listing what each can do, such as listen, connect to resources, or keep a student safe. They match the pairs and check with a partner. The class names which helper to start with and why.

understand-2.objective: Explain why telling a trusted adult is help-seeking, not telling on someone.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a one-sentence reason that distinguishes getting help from tattling.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide reads two short scenarios, one about getting help for a worried child and one about tattling for fun. Students write one sentence explaining the difference and why help-seeking is the caring choice. Pairs compare sentences and agree on the clearest wording.

understand-3.objective: Summarize the steps to ask an adult for help in the student's own words.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student lists the help-seeking steps in order on a strip.
understand-3.sampleTask: Guide shows a help-seeking model: pick a trusted adult, say what you noticed, say how it makes you feel, and ask for help. Students restate the steps in their own words on a sentence strip. Strips post as a class reference for later role-plays.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the help-seeking message changes for an emergency versus an ongoing worry.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student writes how the first move differs for a 911 emergency and a slow-building worry.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives two situations: someone collapses after taking something, and a parent has been drinking more for weeks. Students write what the first move is in each and why one needs 911 and the other needs a trusted adult conversation. Groups compare and post a two-column reference.

apply-1.objective: Use the help-seeking steps in a short scripted talk with a trusted adult.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student delivers a scripted help request to a partner playing the adult.
apply-1.sampleTask: Partners get a script where one plays a counselor. The other reads a help request that names what they noticed and asks for help. They run it twice and switch. Guide taps pairs to read their request to the class.

apply-2.objective: Carry out an unscripted role-play asking a school adult for help with a worry about a family member.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student improvises a help request that names a worry and a clear ask.
apply-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario card describing an ongoing worry at home. One partner plays a trusted school adult; the other asks for help without a script, naming the worry and the ask. Pairs run two rounds and write one sentence on what made the ask clear.

apply-3.objective: Apply help-seeking steps when the first adult is unavailable.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student names a backup adult and re-delivers the request in the role-play.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide sets up a role-play where the first chosen adult is out. The student must pick a backup from a school directory card and deliver the request again. Pairs run it, then write who their backup is and how they would reach that person.

apply-4.objective: Use help-seeking steps in a scenario where the student must keep someone safe right now.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student states the order of actions, including when to call 911, during the role-play.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide presents an urgent scenario where someone took something and is not responding. Students role-play calling 911, stating the location, and getting an adult, in order. Each writes the action sequence and marks the step that comes first.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which detail in a scenario signals that a student should seek help.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines the warning detail in three short scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and underline the detail that means it is time to tell an adult, such as someone getting hurt or a child feeling unsafe. Pairs compare underlines and sort the signals into 'tell an adult soon' and 'call for help now.'

analyze-2.objective: Compare two help-seeking choices to determine which gets the right help faster.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger choice and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives two responses to the same worry: texting a friend versus telling the school counselor. Students decide which gets real help faster and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a class chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break a help-seeking conversation into its parts and find the missing piece.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student labels the parts of a sample request and names what is missing.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a help request that leaves out the clear ask. They label the parts that are present, such as the trusted adult and the worry, and write the missing piece. Pairs rewrite the request so it is complete.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why someone might avoid seeking help and what would lower that barrier.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student lists two barriers and one way to lower each.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups brainstorm reasons a fifth grader might not ask for help, such as fear or shame, and write one way a school could lower each barrier. They build a barrier-and-fix chart and present one row to the class.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a help-seeking role-play against a names-the-worry, names-a-feeling, and makes-an-ask rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a partner's role-play on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students watch a partner's help-seeking role-play and score it on three rows: names the worry, names a feeling, and makes a clear ask. They write one specific note for the strongest row and one for the weakest. Partners discuss one change to try.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which school resource a new student should learn about first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one resource with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which school helper a brand-new fifth grader should learn about first for a hard home problem. Each student writes a claim naming one resource and two reasons. Groups rank the claims and present the top choice with its evidence.

create-1.objective: Generate a quick-reference list of trusted adults and how to reach each.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a personal card listing three adults and a way to reach each.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student fills a wallet-size card with three trusted adults, the role of each, and one way to reach that person. The class reviews what makes a contact reachable. Students keep the card and the Guide stores a backup copy on request.

create-2.objective: Design a poster that shows the steps to seek help for someone affected by another's substance use.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with the help-seeking steps and one school contact.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a hallway poster showing the help-seeking steps in order plus where to find the school counselor or nurse. Drafts get a quick peer check for clear steps. The class votes which posters go up in the hallway.

create-3.objective: Build a role-play script that models seeking help when an adult resists at first.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a script where the student restates the ask after a brush-off.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs write a script where a student asks for help, gets a 'not now,' and restates the ask clearly until heard. The script must show the student staying calm and specific. Pairs perform and the class names the move that kept the ask alive.

create-4.objective: Produce a short class guide on how and when to get help for someone affected by drug or alcohol use.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a one-page guide with steps, contacts, and one worked example.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a one-page guide covering when to seek help, the steps, two school contacts, and a worked example conversation. They draft, peer-review against a checklist, and revise. Final guides go in a class resource binder.

2.3.5.DSDT.5.standardStatement: Identify the various types of resources that are available in the community and online to assist individuals who struggle with alcohol, tobacco, and drug use/abuse (e.g., school nurse, counselor, peer leadership group).
2.3.5.DSDT.5.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four resources that help people who struggle with alcohol, tobacco, or drug use.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four resources on a sticky note.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four people or places that help someone struggling with drugs or alcohol.' Students write four on sticky notes and post them on a board grouped into school, community, and online. The class reads the groups aloud.

understand-1.objective: Describe what each named resource does for someone seeking help.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student matches four resources to their functions on a worksheet.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students get a worksheet with named resources in one column and functions in the other, scrambled. They match each resource to what it does, such as a counselor who listens or a hotline that answers any time. Whole-class review confirms the matches.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a community needs both school-based and outside-of-school resources.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one reason a school resource and one reason an outside resource each matter.
understand-2.sampleTask: Each student writes two sentences: why a school resource helps and why an outside or online resource helps when school is closed. Pairs trade and check that the reasons differ. Two volunteers share a pair that shows both.

understand-3.objective: Group resources into categories and name what defines each category.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts eight resources into categories and labels each category.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort eight resource cards into groups, then write a label and a one-line definition for each group, such as 'people you talk to' or 'places you call.' Pairs compare groupings and resolve any cards that fit two groups.

understand-4.objective: Explain how to judge whether an online resource is trustworthy.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student lists two signs that an online help resource is reliable.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide shows two sample help websites, one official and one not. Students list two signs of a trustworthy site, such as a known organization or a real phone line. Pairs compare lists and the class builds a trust checklist.

apply-1.objective: Use a resource directory to find the right contact for a given need.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student locates and writes the correct contact for one scenario.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a one-page resource directory and a scenario. Each student finds the contact that fits the need and writes the name and how to reach it. Pairs check that the choice matches the scenario.

apply-2.objective: Match three scenarios to the resources that best fit each.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with named resources and a reason for each.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and choose the best resource for each from the class list, adding one reason per choice. Small groups compare and settle any scenario that could fit two resources by explaining the better fit.

apply-3.objective: Apply resource knowledge to plan the first two steps a person should take.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes an ordered two-step plan for a scenario.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives a scenario about a worried student. Each student writes a two-step plan naming who to contact first and what to do next. Pairs check the order makes sense, then the class compares first steps and discusses why order matters.

apply-4.objective: Use multiple resources together when one cannot fully help.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names two resources and how they hand off to each other in the role-play.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide presents a scenario needing more than one resource, such as a counselor who then connects the family to a community program. Students role-play the handoff and write which resource leads and which it connects to. Groups compare handoff plans.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which resource a scenario calls for and which detail signals it.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the resource and underlines the signal in three scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios, name the best resource for each, and underline the detail that pointed them there. Pairs compare and sort the signals into urgent and non-urgent on a class chart.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two resources to determine which fits a specific need better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better-fit resource and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a need and two resources, such as a peer leadership group versus a counselor. Students decide which fits better and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break the local resource map into gaps the community has not covered.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student names one need the current resource list does not meet.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students study the class resource list against a set of common needs. They mark which needs are covered and name one gap, such as no nighttime contact. Groups compare gaps and rank which gap matters most.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why some resources are easier to reach than others.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student lists two factors that make a resource easy or hard to reach.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine four resources and rate each on how easy it is to reach, then list two factors that drive access, such as cost or hours. They build a chart linking factors to access and present one row.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's resource recommendation against a fit, reachability, and trust rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a recommendation on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a partner's resource recommendation for a scenario and score it on fit, reachability, and trust. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which single resource a school should promote most to fifth graders.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one resource with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which resource the school should promote most. Each student writes a claim naming one resource and two reasons tied to access and trust. Groups rank claims and present the top pick with its evidence.

create-1.objective: Generate a class list of community and online resources with contacts.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds two resources with a contact detail to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student adds two resources to a shared class list, including how to reach each. The class reviews for duplicates and checks that each entry has a real contact. The list becomes a posted class reference.

create-2.objective: Design a resource flyer that points fifth graders to two help options.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a flyer naming two resources and how to reach each.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a flyer for the school hallway naming two resources, what each does, and how to reach it. Drafts get a peer check for clarity and accuracy. The class picks flyers to post.

create-3.objective: Build a decision tree that routes a need to the right resource.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a decision tree with at least three branches ending in named resources.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs build a decision tree that starts with a question about the need and branches to named resources, with at least three branches. They test the tree against two scenarios and fix any dead ends. Pairs trade trees and run a third scenario.

create-4.objective: Produce a one-page community resource guide for families.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide listing at least four resources grouped by type with contacts.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a one-page family guide listing at least four resources grouped into school, community, and online, each with a contact and a one-line description. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go to the school office on request.

2.3.5.HCDM.1.standardStatement: Identify conditions that may keep the human body from working properly, and the ways in which the body responds.
2.3.5.HCDM.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four conditions that can keep the body from working properly.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four conditions on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four things that can make the body stop working the way it should.' Students write four, such as infection, injury, lack of sleep, or poor nutrition, on a card. Cards post on a board grouped by whether they come from inside or outside the body.

understand-1.objective: Describe how the body responds to one common condition such as a cut or a cold.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two body responses for one condition.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick a condition from the class board and write two ways the body responds, such as a scab forming or a fever rising. Pairs trade and check that the response matches the condition. The class lists responses next to each condition.

understand-2.objective: Explain why the body's response is a sign it is trying to protect itself.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes a 'this happens because' sentence for one body response.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names body responses like sneezing or swelling. Each student picks one and writes a 'this happens because the body is...' sentence. Pairs compare and check the explanation links the response to protection or repair.

understand-3.objective: Group conditions by whether the body can recover on its own or needs help.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six conditions into self-recovery and needs-care groups.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six condition cards into 'body recovers on its own' and 'needs outside help,' then write a one-line reason for two of them. Pairs compare and discuss any card that could go either way depending on severity.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the same condition can be mild or serious depending on the body's response.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student describes a mild and a serious version of one condition.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one condition, such as an allergic reaction. Students write a mild version and a serious version and note what changed in the body's response. Groups compare and post a 'mild versus serious' chart for the condition.

apply-1.objective: Use a body-systems diagram to label where a condition affects the body.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the affected area on a body diagram for one condition.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a body outline and a condition card. Each student marks the area affected and labels the body's response there. Pairs check the placement against a reference diagram.

apply-2.objective: Match conditions to the body responses they trigger in a scenario set.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three conditions with their responses and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and match each to the body's response, adding one reason per match. Small groups compare and settle any scenario where more than one response fits.

apply-3.objective: Apply knowledge of body responses to decide when a condition needs an adult or a doctor.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student marks three conditions as 'rest,' 'tell an adult,' or 'see a doctor' with a reason.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide gives three conditions with described responses. Students decide whether each needs rest, an adult, or a doctor and write why the response level drove the call. Pairs compare and the class discusses the borderline cases.

apply-4.objective: Use a tracking sheet to log how a body response changes over a few days in a case study.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student records response changes across three days and notes the trend.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a three-day case study of a recovering injury or illness. Students log the body's response each day on a tracking sheet and write whether it is improving or worsening. Pairs compare logs and name the day care was most needed.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which body response in a description signals a problem getting worse.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines the worsening signal in three descriptions.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three short descriptions of body responses and underline the detail that signals the condition is getting worse, such as spreading redness. Pairs compare and sort signals into 'watch' and 'act now.'

analyze-2.objective: Compare two conditions to find what their body responses share.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student lists one shared response and one difference for two conditions.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives two conditions, such as a cold and an allergy. Students list one body response the two share and one that differs. Groups build a Venn diagram and place at least three responses.

analyze-3.objective: Break a body response into a cause-and-effect chain.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps a condition to response chain with at least three links.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students pick a condition and map the chain from cause to body response to outcome, with at least three links, such as germ enters, body raises temperature, germ slows down. Pairs check the chain holds together.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how a person's habits change how the body responds to a condition.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student names one habit and how it strengthens or weakens a body response.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine how habits like sleep, water, and hand-washing affect the body's response to illness. Each student writes one habit and whether it helps or hurts the response. Groups build a habit-and-effect chart and present one row.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's condition-to-response match against an accuracy and reasoning rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a partner's matches on accuracy and reasoning.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students review a partner's condition-to-response matches and score each on accuracy and the reason given. They write one specific note on a strong match and one on a weak match. Partners discuss one correction.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which body response is the most important warning sign for a fifth grader to know.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one response with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which body response a fifth grader should treat as the clearest warning sign. Each student writes a claim naming one response and two reasons tied to safety. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a class word wall pairing conditions with body responses.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds one condition-and-response pair to the word wall.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student adds one condition with its body response to a class word wall card. The class checks for duplicates and accuracy. The word wall stays up as a reference for the unit.

create-2.objective: Design a labeled diagram showing how the body responds to one condition.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a diagram with at least three labeled responses.
create-2.sampleTask: Students draw and label a diagram showing how the body responds to one chosen condition, with at least three labeled parts or steps. A gallery walk picks diagrams that show the response clearly. The class adds the best to a reference folder.

create-3.objective: Build a flowchart that traces a condition from start to the body's response and recovery.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a flowchart with at least four ordered steps.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs build a flowchart tracing one condition from onset through the body's response to recovery or care, with at least four steps. They test the chart against a scenario and fix any gaps. Pairs trade and check each other's flow.

create-4.objective: Produce a mini field guide to common conditions and how the body responds.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide covering at least four conditions with responses and care notes.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a mini field guide covering at least four conditions, each with the body's response and a care note. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go into a class health-reference binder.

2.3.5.HCDM.2.standardStatement: Describe how to prevent the spread of communicable and infection diseases and conditions (e.g., Lyme Disease, influenza)
2.3.5.HCDM.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four ways to prevent the spread of a communicable disease.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four prevention actions on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four things that stop germs from spreading.' Students write four actions, such as hand-washing, covering coughs, vaccines, and staying home when sick, on a card. Cards post grouped by personal habits and shared rules.

understand-1.objective: Describe how a specific disease such as influenza or Lyme spreads from one host to another.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the spread path for one disease in two or three steps.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick influenza or Lyme disease and write how it spreads in two or three steps, such as a tick bite or a cough reaching another person. Pairs trade and check the path is complete. The class posts a spread path for each disease.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a prevention action breaks the chain of spread.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student links one prevention action to the step it blocks.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows a simple chain: germ source, way it travels, new host. Each student picks a prevention action and writes which step it blocks and why. Pairs compare and the class maps actions onto the chain.

understand-3.objective: Group prevention actions by whether they protect the self or protect others.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six actions into protect-self and protect-others.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six prevention actions into 'protects me' and 'protects others,' noting that some do both. They write a one-line reason for two cards. Pairs compare and discuss actions that land in both groups.

understand-4.objective: Explain how prevention differs for a disease spread by contact versus one spread by an insect.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names one prevention action that fits contact spread and one that fits insect spread.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide contrasts influenza, spread by contact, with Lyme, spread by ticks. Students write one prevention action that fits each type and why the methods differ. Groups build a two-column chart linking spread type to prevention.

apply-1.objective: Use a hand-washing or cough-covering checklist correctly in a demonstration.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student performs the steps in order during the demonstration.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide models proper hand-washing or cough-covering. Students follow a checklist and perform the steps in order at their seats or a sink. Pairs check each other against the checklist and mark any step skipped.
materials: soap and water or hand sanitizer

apply-2.objective: Match prevention actions to three everyday scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with the best prevention action and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios, such as a hike in tall grass or a classmate sneezing, and choose the best prevention action with one reason each. Small groups compare and settle any scenario where two actions fit.

apply-3.objective: Apply prevention steps to plan how to stay healthy during a school flu season.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-action plan for flu season.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide sets the context of a flu outbreak at school. Each student writes a three-action plan to lower their risk and protect classmates. Pairs check the plan covers both self and others, then the class compiles a shared action list.

apply-4.objective: Use a tick-check routine in a simulated outdoor scenario.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student names the body areas to check and the order on a body diagram.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide describes a hike through woods. Students mark a body diagram with the areas to check for ticks and number the order of a proper tick check. Pairs compare diagrams against a reference and add any missed areas.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the moment in a scenario where spread could have been prevented.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the prevention point in three short scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios where a disease spreads and mark the point where a prevention action would have broken the chain. Pairs compare marks and name the action that fits each point.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two prevention methods to determine which lowers risk more for a given disease.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger method and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a disease and two prevention methods. Students decide which lowers risk more and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break a disease outbreak story into the links in its chain of spread.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps an outbreak into at least four chain links.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a short outbreak story and map it into chain links: source, exit, travel, entry, new host. They mark where prevention failed. Pairs compare maps and resolve differences.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why some prevention actions work for many diseases and others for only one.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels three actions as broad or specific with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine prevention actions and label each as broad, working against many diseases, or specific to one. Each student writes a reason for one broad and one specific action. Groups build a chart and present one example of each.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a prevention plan against a covers-self, covers-others, and feasible rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a peer's plan on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a partner's flu-season prevention plan and score it on three rows: protects self, protects others, and doable at school. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which prevention action a school should require for everyone.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one action with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which prevention action a school should require schoolwide. Each student writes a claim naming one action and two reasons tied to how well it stops spread. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a class list of prevention reminders for the classroom.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds one prevention reminder to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student writes one clear prevention reminder for the classroom and adds it to a shared list. The class removes duplicates and picks the five most useful to post by the door.

create-2.objective: Design a poster teaching one prevention method step by step.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with the method's steps in order.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a poster that teaches one prevention method, such as proper hand-washing or a tick check, with steps in order and simple drawings. Drafts get a peer check for clear steps. The class posts the clearest in bathrooms or the nurse's office.

create-3.objective: Build a short skit that shows breaking the chain of spread.
create-3.observableCheck: Each group produces a skit where a character takes a prevention action that stops spread.
create-3.sampleTask: Groups write and perform a short skit where germs start to spread and a character's prevention action breaks the chain. The skit must name the action and the step it blocked. The class identifies the prevention point in each skit.

create-4.objective: Produce a prevention guide for families covering at least two diseases.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide with spread paths and prevention steps for two diseases.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a family guide covering at least two communicable diseases, each with how it spreads and steps to prevent it. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go home as a class resource.

2.3.5.HCDM.3.standardStatement: Examine how mental health can impact one’s wellness (e.g., depression, anxiety, stress, phobias).
2.3.5.HCDM.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Name four feelings or conditions that affect mental health, such as stress, anxiety, or sadness.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four feelings or conditions that affect how the mind feels.' Students write four, such as stress, worry, sadness, or fear, on a card. Cards post on a board and the class groups similar ones together.

understand-1.objective: Describe how stress or worry can show up in the body and in daily life.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes two signs of stress, one in the body and one in daily life.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write one way stress shows up in the body, such as a tight stomach, and one way it shows in daily life, such as trouble sleeping. Pairs trade and check the two signs differ. The class lists body and daily-life signs side by side.

understand-2.objective: Explain why mental health is part of overall wellness, not separate from it.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence connecting a mind state to a body or behavior effect.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows the idea that mind and body affect each other. Each student writes one 'when I feel..., then I...' sentence connecting a mental state to an effect on the body or actions. Pairs compare and the class collects examples.

understand-3.objective: Group situations by whether the stress they cause is short-term or ongoing.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six situations into short-term and ongoing.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six situation cards into short-term stress, like a test, and ongoing stress, like a long family worry. They write a one-line reason for two. Pairs compare and discuss any card that could be either.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the same situation can affect two people's mental health differently.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student describes two different reactions to one situation and why they differ.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one situation, such as moving to a new school. Students describe two different ways students might react and explain what makes the reactions differ, like support or past experience. Groups compare and post a 'same event, different reactions' chart.

apply-1.objective: Use a simple coping strategy such as paced breathing during a guided practice.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student performs the breathing steps in time with the guide.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide leads a paced-breathing practice with a count. Students follow along and then rate how their body felt before and after on a 1-to-5 scale. Pairs share which step helped most.

apply-2.objective: Match coping strategies to three stressful scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with a coping strategy and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three stressful scenarios and choose a coping strategy for each, with one reason. Small groups compare and settle any scenario where more than one strategy fits.

apply-3.objective: Apply coping and help-seeking steps to plan a response to a tough week.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a plan with one self-strategy and one trusted adult to tell.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide describes a stressful week with several pressures. Each student writes a plan naming one coping strategy they will use and one trusted adult they could tell. Pairs check the plan has both parts, then the class compiles strategies.

apply-4.objective: Use a feelings tracker over a week-long case study to spot patterns.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student logs daily feelings and names one pattern.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide gives a week-long case study of a student's days. Students log the character's feelings each day on a tracker and name one pattern, such as worry rising before tests. Pairs compare and discuss what could help on the hardest day.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the detail in a scenario that signals stress is affecting wellness.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student underlines the signal in three scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and underline the detail showing stress is hurting sleep, schoolwork, or friendships. Pairs compare and sort signals into 'manageable' and 'time to get help.'

analyze-2.objective: Compare two coping strategies to determine which fits a specific situation better.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the better-fit strategy and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a situation and two coping strategies. Students decide which fits better and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break a stressful event into the chain from trigger to feeling to action.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps a scenario into trigger, feeling, and action links.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students map a scenario into a chain: trigger, the feeling it caused, and the action that followed. They mark where a coping strategy could change the chain. Pairs compare maps.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze how support from others changes how a stressor affects wellness.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student names one support and how it lowers a stressor's impact.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine how supports like friends, family, or a counselor change the impact of a stressor. Each student names one support and how it lowers the impact. Groups build a support-and-effect chart and present one row.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a coping plan against a realistic, healthy, and matched-to-the-stressor rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a peer's plan on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a partner's tough-week plan and score it on three rows: realistic, healthy, and matched to the stressor. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which coping strategy the class should learn to use first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one strategy with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which coping strategy the class should master first. Each student writes a claim naming one strategy and two reasons tied to how well and how easily it works. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a class menu of healthy coping strategies.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds one coping strategy to the class menu.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student adds one healthy coping strategy to a shared class menu, with a one-line note on when to use it. The class removes duplicates and groups strategies by quick versus longer. The menu posts as a reference.

create-2.objective: Design a calm-down corner card that walks a peer through a strategy.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a card with a strategy in clear steps.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a card for a classroom calm-down corner that walks a peer through one strategy step by step. Drafts get a peer check for clarity. The class picks cards to laminate for the corner.

create-3.objective: Build a short story or comic that shows a character noticing stress and coping with it.
create-3.observableCheck: Each student produces a story or comic with a trigger, a feeling, and a healthy response.
create-3.sampleTask: Students write a short story or draw a comic where a character notices stress, names the feeling, and uses a healthy strategy or asks for help. The piece must show all three beats. A gallery walk picks ones that model coping well.

create-4.objective: Produce a wellness guide that links common stressors to coping strategies and supports.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide pairing at least three stressors with strategies and a support.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a wellness guide pairing at least three common stressors with a coping strategy and a place to get support. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go in a class wellness corner.

2.3.5.PS.1.standardStatement: Develop strategies to reduce the risk of injuries at home, school, and in the community.
2.3.5.PS.1.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four common injury risks found at home, school, or in the community.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four risks on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four things that could cause an injury at home, school, or around town.' Students write four, such as wet floors, no helmet, or a hot stove, on a card. Cards post grouped by location.

understand-1.objective: Describe how one specific risk leads to an injury.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the risk-to-injury path for one hazard in two steps.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick a risk from the class board and write how it leads to injury in two steps, such as 'wet floor, someone slips and hits their head.' Pairs trade and check the path. The class lists paths next to each risk.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a safety rule lowers the chance of injury.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student links one safety rule to the risk it reduces.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names safety rules like wearing a helmet or holding the rail. Each student picks one and writes which risk it reduces and how. Pairs compare and the class maps rules onto risks.

understand-3.objective: Group injury risks by whether a person, a place, or equipment causes them.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six risks into person, place, and equipment.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six risk cards into person-caused, place-caused, and equipment-caused, writing a one-line reason for two. Pairs compare and discuss risks that fit more than one cause.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the same activity carries different risks in different settings.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names how one activity's risk changes across two settings.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one activity, such as riding a bike. Students describe its risks at home, at school, and on a busy street, and explain what changed. Groups build a setting-and-risk chart and post one row.

apply-1.objective: Use a safety checklist to spot hazards in a pictured room.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks at least four hazards on a hazard-hunt picture.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a 'hazard hunt' picture of a kitchen or classroom. Students circle at least four hazards and label each. Pairs compare circles against an answer key and add any missed.

apply-2.objective: Match safety strategies to three risky scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with a strategy and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and choose a safety strategy for each with one reason. Small groups compare and settle scenarios where more than one strategy fits.

apply-3.objective: Apply safety strategies to plan how to make one routine activity safer.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a three-step plan to lower the risk of one activity.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns activities like walking to school or cooking a snack. Each student writes a three-step plan that lowers the risk. Pairs check the steps target real hazards, then the class compares plans for the same activity.

apply-4.objective: Use a safety audit to evaluate a real classroom or hallway space.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student records at least three hazards and a fix during the walk-through.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide leads a short walk-through of the classroom or hallway. Students record at least three hazards and a fix for each on an audit sheet. Pairs compare findings and the class lists the top fixes to suggest to the school.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the riskiest moment in a scenario where an injury occurs.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student marks the riskiest point in three scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios and mark the moment where the risk was highest and an injury became likely. Pairs compare and name the strategy that would have helped at that point.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two safety strategies to determine which lowers risk more.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the stronger strategy and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a risk and two strategies. Students decide which lowers it more and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break an injury story into the chain of choices and conditions that led to it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps the story into at least three contributing links.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a short injury story and map at least three links that led to it, such as a missing rule, a hazard, and a hurried choice. They mark the link easiest to break. Pairs compare maps.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why some risks are easy to fix and others need many people to change.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels three risks as quick-fix or needs-others with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine risks and label each as a quick personal fix or one needing others, like the school or town. Each student writes a reason for one of each. Groups build a chart and present one example of each.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a safety plan against a targets-the-hazard, doable, and complete rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a peer's plan on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a partner's activity safety plan and score it on three rows: targets the real hazard, doable, and complete. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which safety change would prevent the most injuries at school.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one change with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which single safety change would prevent the most injuries at school. Each student writes a claim naming one change and two reasons tied to how often the risk happens. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a class list of safety rules for the classroom.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds one safety rule to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student writes one clear classroom safety rule and adds it to a shared list. The class removes duplicates and picks the most important to post. The list becomes the room's agreed rules.

create-2.objective: Design a safety poster warning about one common hazard.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster naming a hazard and its prevention.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a poster about one hazard, showing the risk and how to prevent it. Drafts get a peer check for clarity. The class posts the clearest in the hallway or relevant room.

create-3.objective: Build a redesign of a risky space that removes its top hazards.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a labeled before-and-after drawing fixing at least three hazards.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs pick a risky space, draw it as-is with hazards labeled, then draw a safer version that fixes at least three. They explain each fix in a caption. The class compares redesigns of the same space.

create-4.objective: Produce a home-and-school safety guide for families.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide covering at least four hazards with prevention strategies.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a family safety guide covering at least four hazards across home, school, and community, each with a prevention strategy. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go home as a class resource.

2.3.5.PS.2.standardStatement: Demonstrate first aid procedures for choking, bleeding, burns, poisoning, and stroke (F.A.S.T.).
2.3.5.PS.2.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: Recall what the letters in F.A.S.T. stand for and one first-aid action for choking, bleeding, or burns.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the four F.A.S.T. words and one action on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Write what F.A.S.T. stands for and one first thing to do for a burn, a cut, or choking.' Students write the four words, face, arms, speech, time, and one action on a card. Cards check against a reference poster.

understand-1.objective: Describe what F.A.S.T. helps a person check for and why time matters.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes what each F.A.S.T. letter checks and why speed helps.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students write what each F.A.S.T. letter prompts a person to check and one sentence on why calling for help fast matters. Pairs trade and check all four letters are explained. The class confirms against the reference.

understand-2.objective: Explain why the first step in most first-aid situations is to get an adult or call for help.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one reason calling for help comes first.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide presents the rule that a child's first job is to get help fast. Each student writes one reason why calling an adult or 911 comes before other steps. Pairs compare and the class agrees on the clearest wording.

understand-3.objective: Group emergencies by which first-aid response each one needs.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student matches five emergencies to their first response.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students match five emergencies, choking, bleeding, burn, poisoning, and possible stroke, to the correct first response on a worksheet. Pairs check matches against a reference. The class reviews any mismatches.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the response to a small injury differs from the response to a life-threatening one.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the first move for a minor and a serious version of one injury.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one injury type, such as bleeding. Students write the first move for a small cut and for heavy bleeding and explain what changed. Groups build a 'minor versus serious' chart.

apply-1.objective: Demonstrate the correct first step for choking on a manikin or practice partner with hands shown, not applied.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student shows the correct hand position and calls for help in the practice.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide models the choking response, including calling for help and back blows demonstrated on a manikin. Students practice showing the steps and the call, without applying force to a partner. Guide checks each student's sequence against a checklist.

apply-2.objective: Carry out the F.A.S.T. check in a scripted stroke scenario.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student runs the four F.A.S.T. checks in order and states when to call 911.
apply-2.sampleTask: Partners run a scripted scenario where one shows possible stroke signs. The other walks through face, arms, speech, and time, then states the call to 911. Pairs switch roles and Guide taps pairs to demonstrate.

apply-3.objective: Apply the correct first-aid sequence in an unscripted role-play of bleeding or a burn.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student performs the steps in order, including getting help, without a script.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide draws a scenario card for bleeding or a burn. Students role-play the response in order: get help, then the care steps shown on the reference. Pairs run two rounds and write the sequence they used.

apply-4.objective: Use first-aid steps in a scenario with more than one thing going wrong.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student states which emergency to handle first and why.
apply-4.sampleTask: Guide presents a scenario with two problems at once, such as someone bleeding while another feels faint. Students decide which to handle first, call for help, and run the steps in order. Each writes the priority order and the reason.

analyze-1.objective: Identify which emergency a scenario describes from its signs.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the emergency and underlines the signs in three scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios, name the emergency each describes, and underline the signs that point there, such as a drooping face for stroke. Pairs compare and sort the scenarios by urgency.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two responses to the same emergency to determine which is correct and safe.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the correct response and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives an emergency and two response options, one correct and one risky. Students pick the correct one and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post the safe response with evidence.

analyze-3.objective: Break a first-aid response into ordered steps and find a step out of place.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student reorders a scrambled response and names the misplaced step.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students get a scrambled set of first-aid steps for one emergency. They put the steps in order and name which step a sample responder did out of place. Pairs compare orders against the reference.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why some first-aid actions are safe for a child to do and others are not.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels three actions as child-safe or adult-only with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine first-aid actions and label each as safe for a fifth grader or one to leave for an adult or medic. Each student writes a reason for one of each. Groups build a chart and present one example.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a peer's first-aid demonstration against a correct-order, calls-for-help, and safe rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a partner's demonstration on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students watch a partner's first-aid role-play and score it on three rows: correct order, calls for help, and safe for a child to do. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which first-aid skill every fifth grader should learn first.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one skill with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which first-aid skill is most important for a fifth grader to learn first. Each student writes a claim naming one skill and two reasons tied to how often it is needed and how much it helps. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a quick-reference card of first-response steps for common emergencies.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student produces a card listing the first move for at least three emergencies.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student fills a reference card with the first move for at least three emergencies and the F.A.S.T. words. The class checks cards against the reference for accuracy. Students keep the cards for home.

create-2.objective: Design a step-by-step poster for one first-aid procedure.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster with the procedure's steps in order.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a poster teaching one first-aid procedure with steps in order and simple drawings. Drafts get a peer accuracy check against the reference. The class posts the clearest in the nurse's office or hallway.

create-3.objective: Build a role-play script that walks responders through an emergency from signs to help arriving.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a script with ordered steps and a call for help.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs write a script for one emergency that moves from spotting the signs through calling for help to the correct steps until an adult arrives. The script must keep the steps in order. Pairs perform and the class checks the sequence.

create-4.objective: Produce a first-aid handbook covering all five required emergencies.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a handbook with steps for choking, bleeding, burns, poisoning, and stroke.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a handbook with a page per emergency: signs, first move, and steps, plus the F.A.S.T. guide. They peer-review against a checklist for accuracy and order, then revise. Final handbooks go in a class safety binder.

2.3.5.PS.3.standardStatement: Examine the impact of unsafe behaviors when traveling in motor vehicles and when using other modes of transportation.
2.3.5.PS.3.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four unsafe behaviors people do while traveling in cars, on bikes, or as pedestrians.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four unsafe behaviors on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four unsafe things people do in cars, on bikes, or while walking.' Students write four, such as no seatbelt, no helmet, or crossing without looking, on a card. Cards post grouped by mode of travel.

understand-1.objective: Describe how one unsafe travel behavior raises the chance of injury.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student writes the behavior-to-harm path for one behavior in two steps.
understand-1.sampleTask: Students pick an unsafe behavior and write how it leads to harm in two steps, such as 'no seatbelt, thrown forward in a sudden stop.' Pairs trade and check the path. The class lists paths next to each behavior.

understand-2.objective: Explain why a safety device or rule lowers travel risk.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student links one safety device or rule to the harm it reduces.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide names devices and rules like seatbelts, helmets, and crosswalks. Each student picks one and writes which harm it reduces and how. Pairs compare and the class maps devices to harms.

understand-3.objective: Group travel risks by mode: vehicle passenger, cyclist, or pedestrian.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six risks into the three modes.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six travel-risk cards into passenger, cyclist, and pedestrian, writing a one-line reason for two. Pairs compare and discuss risks that appear in more than one mode.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the impact of an unsafe behavior changes with speed or setting.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student describes how one behavior's risk shifts at low and high speed or in two settings.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives one behavior, such as not wearing a seatbelt. Students describe its impact at low speed in a parking lot and at high speed on a highway and explain the difference. Groups build a setting-and-impact chart.

apply-1.objective: Use a safety checklist to spot unsafe behaviors in a pictured travel scene.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student marks at least four unsafe behaviors in the scene.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives a busy street or car-interior picture. Students circle at least four unsafe behaviors and label each. Pairs compare circles against a key and add any missed.

apply-2.objective: Match safe behaviors to three travel scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with a safe behavior and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three travel scenarios and choose the safe behavior for each with one reason. Small groups compare and settle any scenario where more than one behavior fits.

apply-3.objective: Apply travel-safety rules to plan a safe route or trip.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes a plan naming three safety steps for a given trip.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide assigns a trip, such as biking to a friend's house or riding the bus. Each student writes three safety steps for the trip. Pairs check the steps fit the mode, then the class compares plans for the same trip.

apply-4.objective: Use a checklist to audit a real walking or bus route to school.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student records at least three risks and a safer choice on the audit.
apply-4.sampleTask: Students complete an audit of their own route, real or mapped, recording at least three risks and a safer choice for each. Pairs compare audits and the class lists the most common route risks.

analyze-1.objective: Identify the unsafe behavior most responsible for harm in a scenario.
analyze-1.observableCheck: Each student names the key behavior in three scenarios.
analyze-1.sampleTask: Students read three scenarios where an injury occurs and name the unsafe behavior most responsible. Pairs compare and name the safe behavior that would have changed the outcome.

analyze-2.objective: Compare two travel choices to determine which carries less risk.
analyze-2.observableCheck: Each student picks the lower-risk choice and cites two reasons.
analyze-2.sampleTask: Guide gives a situation and two travel choices. Students decide which carries less risk and cite two reasons. Groups debate and post their pick with evidence on a T-chart.

analyze-3.objective: Break a travel-injury story into the chain of choices that led to it.
analyze-3.observableCheck: Each student maps the story into at least three contributing links.
analyze-3.sampleTask: Students read a travel-injury story and map at least three links that led to it, such as a skipped seatbelt, a distraction, and a sudden stop. They mark the easiest link to break. Pairs compare maps.

analyze-4.objective: Analyze why some travel risks depend on the traveler and others on the environment.
analyze-4.observableCheck: Each student labels three risks as traveler-controlled or environment-set with a reason.
analyze-4.sampleTask: Groups examine travel risks and label each as something the traveler controls or something set by roads, weather, or others. Each student writes a reason for one of each. Groups build a chart and present one example.

evaluate-3.objective: Judge a travel-safety plan against a fits-the-mode, complete, and realistic rubric.
evaluate-3.observableCheck: Each student scores a peer's plan on the three rubric rows.
evaluate-3.sampleTask: Students read a partner's trip safety plan and score it on three rows: fits the mode of travel, complete, and realistic. They write one specific note on the strongest row and one on the weakest. Partners discuss one change.

evaluate-4.objective: Defend which travel-safety rule would prevent the most injuries for kids their age.
evaluate-4.observableCheck: Each student writes a claim naming one rule with two supporting reasons.
evaluate-4.sampleTask: Guide asks which travel-safety rule would prevent the most injuries for fifth graders. Each student writes a claim naming one rule and two reasons tied to how often the risk occurs. Groups rank claims and present the top pick.

create-1.objective: Generate a class list of travel-safety rules for walkers, riders, and passengers.
create-1.observableCheck: Each student adds one travel-safety rule to the class list.
create-1.sampleTask: Each student writes one clear travel-safety rule and adds it to a shared list grouped by mode. The class removes duplicates and picks the most important per mode. The list posts as a reference.

create-2.objective: Design a public-service poster against one unsafe travel behavior.
create-2.observableCheck: Each student produces a poster naming the behavior, the risk, and the safe choice.
create-2.sampleTask: Students design a poster targeting one unsafe travel behavior, showing the risk and the safe choice. Drafts get a peer check for clarity. The class posts the clearest near the school exit or bus area.

create-3.objective: Build a safe-route map that marks hazards and safer crossings.
create-3.observableCheck: Each pair produces a route map marking at least three hazards and a safer path.
create-3.sampleTask: Pairs draw or annotate a route map, marking at least three hazards and showing a safer path or crossing for each. They explain each choice in a caption. The class compares maps of shared routes.

create-4.objective: Produce a travel-safety guide for families covering cars, bikes, and walking.
create-4.observableCheck: Each group submits a guide with safe behaviors for at least three modes.
create-4.sampleTask: Groups build a family travel-safety guide covering at least three modes, each with safe behaviors and one risk to avoid. They peer-review against a checklist and revise. Final guides go home as a class resource.

2.3.5.PS.4.standardStatement: Develop strategies to safely communicate through digital media with respect.
2.3.5.PS.4.subject: chpe

remember-1.objective: List four rules for safe and respectful communication online.
remember-1.observableCheck: Each student writes four rules on a card.
remember-1.sampleTask: Guide posts the prompt: 'Name four rules for staying safe and being respectful online.' Students write four, such as keep passwords private, think before posting, or tell an adult about mean messages, on a card. Cards post grouped by safety and respect.

understand-1.objective: Describe what makes an online message respectful versus hurtful.
understand-1.observableCheck: Each student marks four sample messages as respectful or hurtful and notes why.
understand-1.sampleTask: Guide hands out eight sample messages. Students label each respectful or hurtful and add one word naming the feature, such as kind, rude, or private. Whole-class review names what makes a message respectful.

understand-2.objective: Explain why information shared online can be hard to take back.
understand-2.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence explaining why posts can spread or last.
understand-2.sampleTask: Guide shows how a shared message can be copied or forwarded. Each student writes one sentence explaining why something online is hard to take back. Pairs compare and the class agrees on the clearest wording.

understand-3.objective: Group online actions by whether they protect privacy or risk it.
understand-3.observableCheck: Each student sorts six actions into protects-privacy and risks-privacy.
understand-3.sampleTask: Students sort six online actions into protects privacy and risks privacy, writing a one-line reason for two. Pairs compare and discuss actions that depend on the situation.

understand-4.objective: Explain how the right response to a mean message differs from the response to a stranger's contact.
understand-4.observableCheck: Each student names the first move for each of the two situations.
understand-4.sampleTask: Guide gives two situations: a classmate sends a mean message, and a stranger asks to chat. Students write the first move for each and why the moves differ. Groups build a two-column response chart.

apply-1.objective: Use a respectful-message frame to rewrite a rude message.
apply-1.observableCheck: Each student rewrites one rude message into a respectful one.
apply-1.sampleTask: Guide gives three rude sample messages. Each student rewrites one using a respectful frame that keeps the point but drops the insult. Pairs trade and check the rewrite stays respectful and clear.

apply-2.objective: Match safe responses to three online scenarios.
apply-2.observableCheck: Each student pairs three scenarios with a safe response and a reason.
apply-2.sampleTask: Students read three online scenarios and choose the safe response for each with one reason. Small groups compare and settle any scenario where more than one response fits.

apply-3.objective: Apply digital-safety steps to plan a response to receiving a mean or scary message.
apply-3.observableCheck: Each student writes an ordered plan: do not reply, save it, tell an adult.
apply-3.sampleTask: Guide describes getting a mean or scary message. Each student writes an ordered plan covering not replying, saving the message, and telling a trusted adult. Pairs check the order and the class compares first steps.

apply-4.objective: Use a privacy-settings checklist to set up a sample profile safely.
apply-4.observableCheck: Each student marks at least four safe settings on a sample profile sheet.
